assessments.assessment: From an implementation-planning lens, the passive baseline is not a delivery mechanism at all but a decision to forgo intervention, so classic implementation criteria (actor capacity, sequencing, pilotability) are largely inapplicable except insofar as existing bottlenecks continue unaddressed. The proposal implicitly assumes system stability, but documented evidence shows active, compounding dysfunctions (diagnostic bottlenecks, second-stage service failure, capacity misallocation after centralization) that will continue to run without any monitoring or containment mechanism attached.
opportunities: Could be paired with the monitoring architecture proposal (TP-live-t2) to at least generate feedback data even absent active intervention
strengths: Requires no new actor capacity or coordination, so there is no implementation delivery risk in the conventional sense
Provides an analytically clean counterfactual against which other proposals' marginal effects could be measured, which is itself useful for adaptive management
threats: Absence of any containment mechanism means existing bottlenecks and misallocations documented in the evidence base will persist or compound without any capacity to detect deterioration early
weaknesses: No monitoring or feedback loop is proposed to track whether the documented capacity constraints in diagnostic services worsen (F-live-implementation_planning-03)
No mechanism addresses the already-documented second-stage failure where assessed children do not receive prescribed habilitation and therapy hours (F-live-implementation_planning-04)

The organizational capacity misallocation from the 2013 centralization is left completely unaddressed, allowing the paperwork/therapy imbalance to continue indefinitely (F-live-implementation_planning-05)

assessments.assessment: This proposal directly targets the monitoring and feedback-loop gap that implementation science treats as a precondition for adaptive management. It is well-sequenced as a foundational, low-risk first step (data before structural reform) and is highly pilotable since it does not require frontline behavior change, only administrative/statistical coordination. However, actor capacity to execute decomposition analysis and reconcile divergent series is not established in the evidence, and the proposal's own risk framing acknowledges that political will to act on findings is a separate, unaddressed capability.
opportunities: Could use the 2018 BTMN grading-exemption reform and 2020 readiness rule change as natural experiments for decomposition, as the proposal itself notes, strengthening causal inference
strengths: Directly closes the documented monitoring gap where a single reconciled statistical series does not exist across years and levels (F-live-implementation_planning-01)
Sequencing logic is sound: establishing a shared evidence base before attempting structural or financial reform reduces risk of misdiagnosing the problem
High pilotability since it is a technical/statistical undertaking rather than a frontline service change, limiting failure-containment risk
threats: Even if data reconciliation succeeds, there is no guaranteed downstream actor or process committed to acting on findings, risking a well-executed monitoring system with no policy consequence
weaknesses: No described mechanism for building the technical/statistical capacity or authority needed to reconcile fragmented series and enforce a single public standard

The proposal does not specify safeguards for what happens if decomposition analysis produces contested findings, given documented sharp disagreement over whether reclassification incentives drove SNI growth (F-live-implementation_planning-02, contested)

assessments.assessment: This is the most structurally ambitious proposal and carries the highest implementation risk under this lens. It requires simultaneous redesign of category architecture and gatekeeping capacity, layered onto a system already showing documented bottlenecks (waiting lists, staff turnover, insufficient medical backup) and school-level informal safeguards that are neither audited nor standardized. Sequencing is unclear: it is not specified whether tiered support is piloted regionally before national rollout, and no mechanism is proposed to bring the sole nationwide expert-committee gatekeeper to adequate capacity before or during the transition.
opportunities: A phased or regional pilot approach, rather than the wholesale national redesign implied, could allow the mechanism to be tested against real bottleneck data before full deployment
strengths: Directly targets the documented bottleneck in the identify-assess-serve sequence caused by expert-committee capacity constraints (F-live-implementation_planning-03)

Decoupling support from categorical labeling could reduce the incentive dynamics tied to the 2017-2018 BTMN reclassification pattern, though that pattern remains contested (F-live-implementation_planning-02)
threats: Structural reform layered on top of a system with unresolved second-stage service failures (children not receiving prescribed therapy hours, F-live-implementation_planning-04) risks compounding rather than resolving delivery failure if capacity is not built first
weaknesses: No described sequencing plan for how a single nationwide gatekeeper is restructured or capacity-augmented without disrupting current service continuity

The existing informal, non-uniform school-level self-limiting of SNI intake (F-live-implementation_planning-09) is not addressed as a factor that could undermine or interact unpredictably with a new tiered structure

No safeguard is specified for containing failure if a tiered rollout produces the kind of large persistent regional variation seen elsewhere, given already documented capacity differences across regions

assessments.assessment: The financing-redesign mechanism is conceptually pilotable (formula changes can be tested in subsets of districts) but the proposal itself concedes that no causal evidence links Hungary's specific financing mechanism to the documented identification rise, meaning the intervention rests on an untested incentive theory rather than a diagnosed implementation failure. The existing weak-conditionality problem in supplementary grants is documented, giving some grounding for the accountability component, but sequencing with other reforms (e.g., category redesign in TP-live-t3) is not addressed, risking incoherent or conflicting incentive signals if pursued in isolation.
opportunities: Accountability requirements tying grants to documented service delivery could be combined with the workforce monitoring proposed elsewhere to close both funding and delivery gaps simultaneously
strengths: Targets a documented weak-incentive design flaw where supplementary grants are not tied to strict service-delivery requirements (F-live-implementation_planning-06)
Financing formula changes are administratively pilotable at a formula-design level before national rollout, offering a plausible containment path
threats: Contrasting international experience (noted in the proposal's own risk section) shows funding redesign choices are not universally convergent, meaning transplanting this mechanism without local piloting could produce unanticipated distortions
weaknesses: No causal evidence base ties Hungary's financing mechanism to the identification rise, so the theory of change driving this mechanism is unverified against the country's own data

No mechanism specified for monitoring unintended effects on schools that currently rely on SNI-linked funding, including foundation schools' documented use of SNI status as a funding consideration despite reversed assessment-admission sequencing (F-live-implementation_planning-06)

assessments.assessment: This proposal aligns well with implementation-planning criteria because it targets a documented, specific capacity gap (regional specialist shortages, second-stage service failure) with a phased, regionally targetable mechanism rather than a wholesale structural overhaul. It also explicitly proposes monitoring of whether assessed children receive legally prescribed hours, directly closing part of the feedback-loop gap. However, actor capacity for delivery is uncertain: the proposal does not resolve whether training pipeline throughput can be increased, and the documented organizational drag from the 2013 centralization (time reallocation toward paperwork) suggests added workforce alone may not translate into more therapeutic service time without organizational redesign.
opportunities: Could be sequenced with organizational redesign to counteract the paperwork-time misallocation, increasing the effective yield of new specialist hires
strengths: Directly targets the documented second-stage implementation failure where assessed children do not receive prescribed habilitation and therapy hours (F-live-implementation_planning-04)
Regional targeting addresses documented capacity gaps such as zero-specialist districts in the northeast, aligning mechanism with diagnosed need rather than uniform national rollout
Includes an explicit monitoring component (tracking whether legally prescribed hours are delivered), partially closing the feedback-loop gap
threats: Without addressing the underlying organizational time-allocation problem, workforce expansion risks being absorbed into non-therapeutic administrative functions rather than closing the service gap it targets
weaknesses: Does not address the organizational-design driver of capacity misallocation from the 2013 centralization, where staff time shifted toward assessment/paperwork rather than therapy, which could blunt the effect of new hires (F-live-implementation_planning-05)

No mechanism is proposed to verify that increased training enrollment converts into practicing workforce, a gap acknowledged in the proposal's own risk framing but not resolved with a specific pipeline intervention

assessments.assessment: This is the broadest, most cross-domain proposal, requiring coordination across multiple actor types (school-choice policy, network planning, private/church sector oversight, legal compliance bodies) with no single implementing authority clearly identified. It correctly identifies a structural precondition (free-school-choice-driven segregation) into which SEN identification enters, and it proposes using a documented precedent (the 2020 readiness rule change) as a model for anticipatory impact assessment, which is a reasonable monitoring innovation. However, the proposal's own risk section acknowledges strong documented institutional resistance to structural anti-segregation reform, and no sequencing or pilot pathway is specified for overcoming this resistance before full-scale coordination is attempted.
opportunities: A narrower pilot focusing solely on extending safeguards to free school choice and selective tracks, before attempting full cross-domain coordination, could test feasibility with lower failure-containment risk
strengths: Grounds the mechanism in an established structural driver of segregation (free school choice plus residential mobility) rather than treating SEN identification as an isolated phenomenon (F-live-implementation_planning-08)
Proposes using the documented 2020 readiness rule change as a template for anticipatory cross-domain impact assessment, offering a concrete monitoring innovation grounded in precedent
threats: Documented historical precedent of institutional resistance to structural anti-segregation reform suggests a high risk of stalled or diluted implementation absent a phased, politically sequenced rollout
weaknesses: No single coordinating actor or authority is specified for the cross-domain review mechanism, leaving unclear who has the capacity or mandate to execute it

The proposal does not address how documented informal school-level gatekeeping behavior (self-limiting SNI intake) would interact with or undermine a centrally coordinated anti-segregation mechanism (F-live-implementation_planning-09)
